title: Honeycomb
subtitle: British cinder toffee with bicarbonate of soda
excerpt: Honeycomb is made from sugar and golden syrup cooked to hard crack stage, then taken off the heat and hit with bicarbonate of soda, which causes an immediate and violent foam. The mixture is poured at once (don't touch it) and within minutes it sets into a brittle, caramel-tasting slab riddled with air bubbles. It shatters when you snap it. It is the inside of a Crunchie bar, and it is easy to make once you understand that the bicarbonate foam can't be rushed or stirred down.
sourceNotes: Honeycomb toffee (also called cinder toffee, sponge toffee, or hokey pokey) is made throughout the British Isles, Australia, and New Zealand. The technique of aerating hot toffee with bicarbonate of soda produces the characteristic bubble structure. The Cadbury Crunchie bar (first produced in 1929) popularised this confection industrially; the homemade version is identical in structure and has been made in British kitchens since at least the nineteenth century. In Australia and New Zealand it is known as hokey pokey.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 slab (about 20 × 20 cm), breaks into irregular pieces
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps for 5 days in an airtight tin at room temperature. Humidity is the enemy — it goes sticky in a damp environment.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: no oven required; cooked on the hob to hard crack stage

glossaryTerms.definition: A stage in sugar cookery reached at around 149 to 154 degrees C (300 to 310 degrees F), where the sugar solution contains almost no water. Dropped into cold water, it forms rigid, brittle threads that snap cleanly. Hard crack is required for honeycomb, turrón, pulled sugar, and boiled sweets. At lower temperatures the candy will be chewy or sticky; above 154 degrees C it begins to caramelise and eventually burn. A sugar thermometer is the reliable method.

content.text: before the bicarbonate goes in. Once you add the bicarb, do not stir; just fold it in three or four times with a wooden spoon and pour immediately. Stirring collapses the bubbles. The slab will expand as it cools; give it plenty of room. Do not touch it while it is setting.

Line a 20 × 20 cm tin or a baking tray with lightly oiled parchment; the honeycomb will expand so leave plenty of room. Measure 1.5 tsp bicarbonate of soda into a small bowl and sift it; have a wooden spoon ready. Combine 200 g caster sugar and 3 tbsp golden syrup in a large, deep, heavy-based saucepan. The pan should be much bigger than the mixture; the foam expands dramatically. Heat over medium heat, stirring until the sugar dissolves. Stop stirring and cook to 149 degrees C on a sugar thermometer; hard crack stage. The mixture will be a deep amber. Remove from the heat. Working quickly, tip in the sifted bicarbonate and fold gently three or four times; the mixture will foam violently. Pour immediately into the prepared tin without spreading or pressing. Leave completely undisturbed for 20 minutes until set hard and cold. Break into pieces with a rolling pin or your hands.

items.problem: Honeycomb is dense with very few bubbles
items.solution: The bicarb was stirred in rather than folded, or stirred after pouring. Add the bicarb off the heat, fold two to three times only, and pour immediately without touching again. Also check the bicarbonate is fresh — old bicarb loses its reactivity.

items.problem: Honeycomb is sticky and does not set brittle
items.solution: The toffee did not reach hard crack stage. It needs 149 degrees C minimum. Also ensure it is stored airtight — humidity causes honeycomb to absorb moisture and go sticky within hours.

The heat was too high. Cook over medium rather than high heat and use a thermometer rather than judging by colour alone. The colour at hard crack is amber but not mahogany — if it looks very dark, it is on the edge of burning.
